title: Staffing
summary: Staffing is finding the right people for the right job. It includes manpower planning, recruitment, selection, training, and development.
Staffing as part of Human Resource Management (HRM) — concerned with obtaining, utilising, and maintaining a satisfied workforce.
Recruitment = searching for and attracting candidates (positive process). Selection = choosing the best candidate (negative process — eliminates unfit).
Internal sources: Transfers, Promotions. External sources: Direct recruitment, Advertisements, Employment exchanges, Campus placement, Labour contractors.
Selection process: Preliminary screening → Selection tests → Interview → Reference check → Selection decision → Medical exam → Job offer → Contract.
Training = improving skills for current job. Development = preparing for future roles.
Training methods: On-the-job (Apprenticeship, Internship, Job rotation) and Off-the-job (Vestibule training, Classroom lectures, Case study, Conferences).

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: Recruitment = invitation (come join us). Selection = rejection (we choose the best). If the question says 'WE ARE HIRING' poster → Recruitment. If it says 'tests, interviews, medical exam' → Selection. 'Learning in a duplicate factory setup' → Vestibule training. 'Learning under a master craftsman' → Apprenticeship.

sections.title: Meaning of Staffing

definition.text: The management function of finding the right person for the right job, with the right qualifications, at the right time.

sections.content: Staffing is the process of filling positions in the organisational structure through manpower planning, recruitment, selection, placement, training, development, and compensation.

sections.title: Staffing Process
points: 1. Manpower Planning: Estimating the number and type of people needed.
2. Recruitment: Searching for and attracting potential candidates.
3. Selection: Choosing the most suitable candidate.
4. Placement & Orientation: Introducing the selected person to the job and organisation.
5. Training & Development: Improving current skills and preparing for future roles.
6. Performance Appraisal: Evaluating employee performance.
7. Compensation: Determining pay and benefits.

sections.title: Recruitment — Sources
points: Internal Sources:
• Transfers: Moving an employee from one job/department to another at the same level.
• Promotions: Moving to a higher position with more responsibility and pay.
External Sources:
• Direct Recruitment: Notice at factory gate for casual vacancies.
• Advertisements: In newspapers, magazines, online portals.
• Employment Exchanges: Government agencies maintaining lists of job seekers.
• Campus Placement: Recruiting from educational institutions.
• Labour Contractors: Supply workers especially for construction, mining.
• Recommendations: From existing employees or acquaintances.

sections.title: Selection Process
points: 1. Preliminary Screening: Shortlisting applications based on basic qualifications.
2. Selection Tests: Intelligence tests, Aptitude tests, Personality tests, Trade tests.
3. Employment Interview: Face-to-face assessment of candidate's suitability.
4. Reference & Background Check: Verifying information provided by the candidate.
5. Selection Decision: Final choice of the most suitable candidate.
6. Medical Examination: Ensuring physical fitness for the job.
7. Job Offer: Appointment letter with terms and conditions.
8. Contract of Employment: Formal agreement between employer and employee.

sections.title: Training vs Development
points: Training: Process of improving skills and knowledge for the current job. Short-term. Specific and job-oriented.
Development: Process of overall growth for future roles. Long-term. General and career-oriented.

sections.title: Training Methods
points: On-the-Job Methods (learn while working):
• Apprenticeship: Learning under a master worker for a prescribed period. Used in trades like plumbing, electrician, craftwork.
• Internship: Students work temporarily in a real organisation to gain practical experience.
• Job Rotation: Moving employees through different jobs to broaden their understanding.
Off-the-Job Methods (learn away from workplace):
• Vestibule Training: Training in a simulated work environment with same equipment as actual factory — learning without disrupting production.
• Classroom Lectures: Theory-based instruction in a formal setting.
• Case Study: Analysing real or hypothetical business situations.
• Conference/Seminars: Group discussions with experts.

tables.title: Recruitment vs Selection
tables.tip: Very common board question. Know all differences.
rows: Pick the most suitable from the pool

tables.title: Training vs Development
tables.tip: Know the basic differences — comes as 3-mark question.

questions.answer: Positive process — it invites and attracts candidates to apply.

questions.text: Training in a duplicate factory setup is called:
questions.answer: Vestibule Training — uses same equipment as the real workplace but in a separate simulated environment.

questions.text: Explain any three external sources of recruitment.
questions.answer:
(i) Advertisements: Publishing job openings in newspapers, websites, and job portals to reach a wide audience.
(ii) Campus Placement: Companies visit educational institutions to recruit fresh graduates directly.
(iii) Employment Exchanges: Government agencies that maintain databases of job seekers and connect them with employers.

(1) Recruitment searches for candidates; Selection chooses the best.
(2) Recruitment is a positive process (invites); Selection is negative (eliminates).
(3) Recruitment creates a pool of applicants; Selection picks one from the pool.
(4) Recruitment precedes selection in the staffing process.

questions.text:
A company hires workers through a 'WE ARE HIRING' poster at the factory gate for temporary positions. Later, selected workers undergo training where they operate machines in a separate training room that replicates the factory floor.

Identify the source of recruitment and training method used.
questions.answer:
1. Direct Recruitment: Posting a hiring notice at the factory gate to attract casual/temporary workers is direct recruitment — an external source.

2. Vestibule Training: A separate training room replicating the factory floor with the same machines is vestibule training — workers learn without disrupting actual production.

pyqs.text: Explain 'Apprenticeship' and 'Vestibule Training' as methods of training.
pyqs.answer:
Apprenticeship: The worker learns under the guidance of a master craftsman for a prescribed period. Used in trades like plumbing, carpentry, and printing.

Vestibule Training: Workers are trained in a simulated work environment with the same equipment as the actual workplace. Useful when on-the-job training might disrupt production.

related_chapters.title: Organising
related_chapters.reason: Staffing fills the roles created by organising

related_chapters.title: Directing
related_chapters.reason: After staffing, employees need to be directed
